mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Uruguay on?
mapQuestions.answer: South America. Uruguay sits on the Atlantic coast, sandwiched between two giants: Argentina to the west and Brazil to the north 🌎

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Uruguay?
mapQuestions.answer: Montevideo, on the Río de la Plata estuary. About half of all Uruguayans (around 1.7 million) live in or around it.

mapQuestions.question: What language does Uruguay speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Spanish, with a sing-song Italian-influenced accent like Argentina's. You will hear lots of Italian words mixed in because so many Italian families moved here in the late 1800s.

mapQuestions.question: Is Uruguay big or small?
mapQuestions.answer: Small! Uruguay is the second-smallest country in South America (after Suriname). You can drive from one side to the other in a single day. About 3.5 million people live here.

mapQuestions.question: How many cows live in Uruguay?
mapQuestions.answer: About 12 million, that's nearly FOUR cows for every Uruguayan person. Uruguay produces more beef per person than almost any other country on Earth.

foods.name: Asado
foods.description: The long slow BBQ, even more central to Uruguayan life than to Argentine. Ribs, sausages and steak cooked over wood embers on a "parrilla" grill. Sunday lunch, every Sunday.

foods.name: Chivito
foods.description: The national sandwich: steak, ham, melted mozzarella, fried egg, bacon, lettuce, tomato, mayo, all stacked into one giant roll. Built to share (or not).

foods.name: Empanada
foods.description: Beef, ham and cheese, or "cuatro quesos" (four cheeses). Eaten everywhere, at home, at football, after the beach.

foods.name: Dulce de leche
foods.description: Caramel made by slowly boiling milk and sugar. Uruguayans put it on toast, pancakes, ice-cream, biscuits. Uruguay claims dulce de leche was invented here (Argentina disagrees).

foods.name: Pizza al cuadrado + fainá
foods.description: Thick Italian-immigrant-style pizza by the slab, often eaten with a chickpea-flour pancake called "fainá" laid on top. A Montevideo classic with strong Italian roots.

foods.name: Helado artesanal
foods.description: Artisan ice-cream parlours descended from Italian gelaterias are everywhere. Dulce de leche, granizado, sambayón flavours. A Montevideo summer evening means a giant cone with two scoops.

scavengerItems.label: An adult walking around with a mate gourd and a thermos under their arm (Uruguay drinks more mate than any country on Earth)
The Río de la Plata coastline (it LOOKS like the ocean but is actually freshwater)
A working "estancia" (cattle ranch) with gauchos on horseback
A Uruguay national football jersey (Uruguay has won the World Cup twice, 1930 and 1950!)
White Atlantic beaches stretching for miles (Punta del Este, Cabo Polonio)
A 1920s Italian-style boulevard or palm-lined façade in Montevideo
A "parrilla" (BBQ pit) firing up with ribs and chorizos in the late afternoon
An "ombú" tree, Uruguay and Argentina's huge umbrella-shaped national tree
A retro 1950s city bus, or any vintage car still being driven (Uruguayans keep cars running for decades)
The "Hand in the Sand" sculpture rising out of the beach at Punta del Este
A capybara at a riverside (the world's biggest rodent, and the calmest)
A "candombe" drum group, Afro-Uruguayan drumming brought by enslaved people centuries ago

geoMatches.description: The capital. Low-rise, Italian-influenced, with palm-lined boulevards, a 22 km waterfront promenade ("la rambla") and one of the safest, friendliest reputations in the region.
A UNESCO old town founded by the Portuguese in 1680. Cobbled colonial streets, lighthouse, ferry hop across the river from Buenos Aires.
The South American "Saint-Tropez". Glitzy Atlantic beaches, the famous "Hand in the Sand" sculpture rising out of the beach, and a buzzing summer scene.
A wild, off-grid Atlantic village that you can only reach by 4x4 over the sand dunes. No electricity, no running water, a lighthouse, and one of the biggest sea lion colonies in South America.
Flat rolling cattle country covering most of the country's interior. Endless horizon, gauchos on horseback, eucalyptus windbreaks. Calm and big.
A northern town near the Brazilian border with natural hot-spring complexes. The Termas del Daymán are the most famous, a series of thermal pools at different temperatures.

senses.smell: Wood-smoke from a parrilla on a Sunday afternoon, leather from a gaucho saddle, eucalyptus on the pampas wind, Atlantic salt spray, ground yerba mate herb being prepared in a gourd…
senses.hear: Argentine-sounding Spanish with an Italian-Uruguayan lilt, "¡Tá!" and "Dale" called between friends, the slurp of mate through a metal straw, gulls over Montevideo's port, distant candombe drum patterns…
senses.taste: Smoky grilled beef, cool dulce de leche on everything sweet, bitter yerba mate passed around a circle, thick Italian-style ice-cream, fresh empanadas straight from the oven…
senses.feel: The warmth of a metal mate gourd cradled in your palm, soft Atlantic sand under bare feet, cool pampas wind whipping across your face, the hot rim of a parrilla grill on a winter night, sea lion fur (if you're very lucky)…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why does every Uruguayan carry a thermos and a gourd?
stories.body: Uruguay drinks more mate (MAH-tay) per person than any other country in the world, over 8 kilos of dried yerba mate leaves per person per year. Mate is a hot herbal tea that you drink through a metal straw (a "bombilla") from a hollow gourd. You pack the gourd with ground yerba leaves, pour hot (but not boiling!) water on top, and sip. When the water finishes, you refill from your thermos. Adults carry their gourd, straw and thermos with them EVERYWHERE, to work, to the beach, on buses, on bikes. It is shared in a circle: you drink the whole gourd, then pass it back to the "cebador" (preparer) who refills and passes it to the next person. There is even a special bus seat with a hole for your thermos.

stories.question: Uruguay won the FIRST World Cup. How?
stories.body: In 1930, the very first FIFA World Cup was held in Uruguay, picked because Uruguay had won Olympic gold in football in 1924 and 1928, and because it was the country's 100th anniversary. Thirteen countries took part. The final was Uruguay vs Argentina, played in the brand new Centenario Stadium in Montevideo, in front of 68,000 fans. Uruguay won 4-2 and the country exploded with celebration. Twenty years later, in 1950, Uruguay won again, this time in Brazil, beating the hosts 2-1 in the deciding game, one of the biggest upsets in football history (Brazilians call it the "Maracanazo"). For a country of fewer than 4 million people, having two World Cups on the trophy shelf is extraordinary.

stories.question: What is the chillest animal in South America?
stories.body: The capybara. They look like giant guinea pigs the size of a Labrador, weigh up to 65 kg, and they are the largest rodents on Earth. They live in groups near rivers and wetlands, and they have a reputation for being the calmest animals in the world, birds perch on them, monkeys sit on them, alligators ignore them, dogs play with them. Capybaras are often seen napping in groups of ten or twenty along the banks of Uruguayan and Argentine rivers, with little marsh birds standing on their backs. In recent years they have become a kind of internet celebrity for being so unbothered by literally everything.

stories.question: Why are there four cows for every person?
stories.body: When Spanish settlers brought the first cattle to the Río de la Plata in the 1500s, the flat grasslands of what is now Uruguay turned out to be PERFECT cattle country. Endless grass, mild weather, no big predators. The herds bred wild and exploded. Over the next 300 years, Uruguayan beef became one of the country's biggest exports. Today Uruguay has around 12 million cattle for 3.5 million people, about 3.4 cows per person, the highest ratio of any country. Almost all of them are grass-fed and free-roaming. That's why an asado in Uruguay tastes the way it does, and why Sunday lunch is treated almost like a national religion.

stories.question: What is candombe drumming?
stories.body: Candombe is a drumming, singing and dancing tradition that came to Uruguay with enslaved African people, mostly from Angola and the Congo, in the 1700s. Three different-sized drums, chico (small), repique (medium) and piano (big), are played together to create one rolling, polyrhythmic pattern that pushes you to move. Every February, candombe groups (called "comparsas") parade through the streets of Montevideo for "Las Llamadas" (The Callings), in some of the biggest carnival nights in the world. UNESCO put candombe on its list of "intangible cultural heritage" in 2009. It is one of the most important Afro-Uruguayan traditions, alive and growing.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Candombe survived for centuries because people kept teaching it to the next generation. What family things do you want to keep teaching forward?

convoQuestions.question: Have you tried mate yet? What did you think of the taste?
Best food in Uruguay so far?
Beach, pampas, city, hot springs, which has felt the most "Uruguay"?
Uruguay is small but punches well above its weight. What does it remind you of from home?
Uruguay won the first ever World Cup. How would you feel if your country had won that?
Candombe drums sound completely different from samba or salsa. What music has stuck in your head here?

wordSearch.hint: Uruguayan foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Hereford / Aberdeen Angus cattle
animals.description: Endless herds across the pampas. Uruguay has nearly four cows per person.

animals.name: Gaucho horse
animals.description: On every working estancia, ridden by gauchos in flat hats and bombachas (loose riding trousers).

animals.name: South American sea lion
animals.description: Huge colony at Cabo Polonio and the Isla de Lobos off Punta del Este, audible from kilometres away.

animals.name: Capybara
animals.description: World's biggest rodent. Calm, sociable, often napping by rivers and wetlands.

animals.name: Rhea (ñandú)
animals.description: A large flightless bird like a small ostrich, running across the pampas.

animals.name: Southern right whale
animals.description: Off the Atlantic coast at La Paloma and Cabo Polonio between July and November.
